title: River Flows in You
source: freemidi.org (full-length solo-piano transcription, 47 measures, 2:45; replaced the 617-byte 19-measure bitmidi uploads/62390 excerpt, 2026-07-09 data-audit follow-up)

musicalLanguage.description: Written in 2001 by the Korean pianist Yiruma, 'River Flows in You' became the internet era's gateway piano piece — hugely searched, endlessly covered, and beloved because it sounds harder than it is. This library carries a full-length solo-piano transcription: 47 measures of 4/4 at 72 BPM, 814 notes across about two and three-quarter minutes. The content-based analysis confirms the stated A major emphatically — statedKeyFit 0.856, with A major also the detected key at a 0.276 margin — and the confidence-gated chord windows certify A major on 18% of measures, E major on 8%, and a single F#m window: the I, V, and vi of the key. The rest of the piece's famous four-chord loop stays below the confidence gate in this flowing arpeggiated texture, so the certified windows are deliberately read with restraint. The piece is built from a handful of recurring cells — a 10-note left-hand figure alone accounts for five separate statements — which is exactly why it rewards pattern-first practice over bar-by-bar grinding.
musicalLanguage.structure: An arch. Measures 1-4 are a bare right-hand introduction (measures 2 and 4 carry just 2 onsets each, and measure 2 is the left hand's only full-measure rest); the main statement runs 5-20, opened by the section lens's strongest interior boundary (novelty 2.969 at measure 5, where the left-hand arpeggios enter); a restatement spans 21-29, opened by measure 21 returning identical to measures 2 and 4 — the intro gesture reused as a section pivot; the climax zone is 30-37, holding the file's busiest measures (31 and 33 at 26 onsets; 17, 30, and 32 at 25); a wind-down covers 38-42; and the coda, 43-47, thins to a single onset in each hand at measure 47.
keyMoments: Measures 1-4: the piece opens as near-solo right-hand gestures — measures 2 and 4 hold only 2 onsets — and they teach that an opening can be a whispered contract with the listener rather than a statement of full texture.

Measure 5: the left hand enters and the section lens registers its strongest interior novelty score (2.969) anywhere in the piece — a clean demonstration that texture change, not harmony change, is what articulates this music's form.

The 10-note left-hand cell first heard at measures 6-8 returns at 10-12, 23-25, 27-29, and 43-45 — five statements covering roughly a quarter of the left hand's content, a model example of how minimal materials sustain a whole piece.

Measures 30-37, the climax: the 27-note left-hand span from measures 17-20 returns at 30-33 and again at 34-37 while the right hand peaks the piece's density at measures 31 and 33 (26 onsets) — intensity built by stacking familiar cells, not by new material.

Measure 47 closes the piece with one onset per hand — after the m43-45 return of the opening left-hand cell, the music dissolves rather than cadencing emphatically, a model of an ending as a release of texture instead of a final exclamation.
teachingGoals: Recognize the I-V-vi skeleton the analysis actually certifies — A major (18% of measures), E major (8%), and one F#m window — and practice hearing those three functions as the gravitational centers of the whole piece.

Master the two recurring left-hand spans before anything else: the 10-note cell (measures 6-8, five statements in all) and the 27-note span (measures 17-20, returning at 30-33 and 34-37) — together they are most of the accompaniment.
Practice absolute evenness in the arpeggiated texture: at 72 BPM the sixteenth-note flow must run like water, and any lump in the rhythm is instantly audible in music this transparent.
Learn the section map as practice units — boundaries at measures 5, 21, 30, 38, and 43 — and treat each unit as one gesture, since the section lens shows the piece thinks in these spans.

Build dynamic pacing directly from the density profile: 2-onset opening measures, a 26-onset peak at measures 31 and 33, and a 1-onset-per-hand final measure — the dynamic arc is written into the note counts.
styleTips: Change the sustain pedal with each harmony and never let adjacent chords blur — this texture shimmers when the pedal is clean and turns to mud when it isn't.

Float the right-hand melody above the arpeggios with a touch differential: arm weight into melody fingers, feather-light accompaniment, so the tune sings from C#4 territory up to its E6 peak at measure 14, riding above the left hand's E2 foundation.
Keep the tempo patient at 72 BPM — this piece is a slow river, and rushing the sixteenth notes collapses the calm that makes it work.
Cap the dynamics around mezzo-forte even at the measure 31-33 climax; the piece lives between pianissimo and mf, and its intensity should come from texture density, not a forced crescendo.
Let measure 47 truly dissolve: play the final onsets as if the music continues somewhere out of earshot — don't place a firm final accent, and let the pedal carry the last sound into silence.
